### Compacted User 1
I wrote some random notes inside [DRAFT_NOTES.md](docs/DRAFT_NOTES.md) for the features I want in this app. I want to discuss with you to clarify, extend, incrementally turn this into full well-thought specs. I need guidance, questions, I don't want you to make any decision blindly, rather ask me, help me take it further by sollicitating me on what matters, crarifying unclear points, etc. We don't want to leave ANYTHING aside.
### Compacted User 2
1. Yes this is just an out-of-scope note for later
2. We can keep it single-user for now
3. I believe git-tracked files is enough; database seems confusing and inconvenient for now
4. I'm thinking more every agent run rather than every mutation; whether it's inside a chat, inside a workflow: every "atomic" piece of work would be commited as an operation in the history
5. Yes, this means we would have to attach some stuff that defines the runtime effects; I'm not sure what is convenient here. In any case we want real tangible elements tied to workflows, and common understandable schemas, e.g. we know how to cancel a cron correctly, and a workflow with a cron knows its id and stuff
6. Integrations/signals should only be for the origin workspace
7. We should keep workspaces scoped, with no cross-workspace access. This is scope creep.
8. I don't understand can you make the question more specific, and easier for me to answer?
9. I feel like this would be useful to unify; but that's a big question, I'd rather we get the full picture, and we decide together with your argumented insight; we want to consider all cases, have a good overview of all the ways to use it, where they integrate, so we make a really well-thought decision on that
10. Overall orchestration is that an entry always starts from the "base" agent (with knowledge on tools and when to use them), and whenever it wants to use a tool we inject relevant context. When an agent is triggered by a workflow, a signal, a cron, it has a special additional context that explains what is triggering it, roughly what is interesting for it, and what it has available. We do definitely want to encourage any main agent that is being triggered, whatever the way, to use subagents to delegate and preserve its context for orchestration.
11. This is something that is already a parameter in session chat (there is a button for auto-approving). But we want to add some settings for this, to be able to set a default setting for a new chat of every kind. I'm not sure if there are some settings about that already. But basically we want to let the user select this default allowance for various kinds of chats depending on where they are triggered from, which can always be updated on the flight once the workflow (=> chat) started
12. We don't have any guardrails currently, except roughly:
a. Only the origin workspace uses integrations, so they only run in this folder, and sessions here don't have write access outside this folder
b. what would be great would be to run sandboed; this is something we want to take a not of, that we WILL implement at some point because it's important, but that is scope creep right now
c. the agents never have access to API keys for integrations; it's an app setting and they can use it but they could never actually read these
d. any write action to an external integration will require approval, not as in "the agent will ask" but as in if some workflow has for purpose to generate some outbound stuff, it will add an entry in some tab in some view (where it's the most relevant to put) with everything ready, but asking for confirmation
13. Should be the timezone of the user, missed run we can maybe just notify; we should actually have a tab that is tangeant to the history but not the history, where have cron runs, signals runs; we could display missed ones. We need to figure out how to best separate between history/cron and signals runs/approval requests. Retry policy should have some defaults and could be customized in the cron
14. This is for us to decide, that's also why we're discussing
15. I think history is better, but we need to figure out with the other similar views
16. No need to worry about that we're getting the full picture here

I'm wondering what the best course of action would be for commits/checkpoints because there is one annoying thing: if the user modifies something we can't keep some simple AI commit mechanisms. Or we could just have it so when AI starts a task, if there is anything uncommitted, it just creates a commit that is labeled as user-changes commit. BUT the issue is, what if two agents are doing something at the same time? Maybe we should just have agent create a new branch when they do something, then merge into main and remove their branch? Does this also remove the user uncommitted files issue?

not commit -> operation, and commited by the AI with a short message

Again, don't rush anything, don't rush into writing a spec, don't leave anything aside, in the opposite, strive for us to have it all figure out correctly, with high quality design and UX.
### Compacted User 3
How would the single-writer per workspace rule work? I'm worried this will be too complex and introduce flakiness.

My understanding right now is:
- we could do that and queue agents but this seems like breaking good ux
- in any case nothing prevents the user from editing the files directly on their machine
- this would be flaky between ai edits and user edits

A note is that there might be conflicts when trying to revert a checkpoint, in such case we should let the agent do the reconciliation.

<details>

```md
<permissions instructions>
Filesystem sandboxing defines which files can be read or written. `sandbox_mode` is `danger-full-access`: No filesystem sandboxing - all commands are permitted. Network access is enabled.
Approval policy is currently never. Do not provide the `sandbox_permissions` for any reason, commands will be rejected.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

<collaboration_mode># Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.
</collaboration_mode>
```

</details>

# AGENTS.md instructions for /Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin

<INSTRUCTIONS>
- To regenerate the JavaScript SDK, run `./packages/sdk/js/script/build.ts`.
- ALWAYS USE PARALLEL TOOLS WHEN APPLICABLE.
- The default branch in this repo is `dev` and it is the integration base for fork work.
- Track upstream changes from `upstream/dev` as read-only input only.
- Never commit directly on `upstream/dev` and never target it as the base branch for fork feature work.
- Local `main` ref may not exist; use `dev` or `origin/dev` for diffs.
- Prefer automation: execute requested actions without confirmation unless blocked by missing info or safety/irreversibility.

## Repo Context (Read First)

- Start with [docs/specs/00.project-direction.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/00.project-direction.md) for the fork's current direction and scope boundaries.
- Follow [docs/specs/GUIDE.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/GUIDE.md) for the required phase `.spec`/`.log` workflow.
- Keep work scoped to native desktop goals unless a phase spec explicitly expands scope.
- Treat `docs/specs/00.project-direction.md` as a living baseline: when a phase changes direction or scope boundaries, update it in the same phase/PR.
- Direction-change triggers include new defaults, guardrails, namespace boundaries, startup behavior, import policy boundaries, or product naming rules.
- Keep `docs/specs/00.project-direction.md` concise and direction-only: summarize stable outcomes and link phases; keep implementation detail in phase `.spec`/`.log`.

## Fork Sync (On Demand)

- Only load [docs/sync-fork/GUIDE.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/sync-fork/GUIDE.md) when the task explicitly asks to sync this fork with upstream.
- Do not load `docs/sync-fork/GUIDE.md` for unrelated implementation tasks.

## Commits

- Use Conventional Commits format: `<type>(<scope>): <description>` (scope optional).
- Keep commit messages concise and technical.
- Do not use emojis in commit title or body.

## Style Guide

### General Principles

- Keep things in one function unless composable or reusable
- Avoid `try`/`catch` where possible
- Avoid using the `any` type
- Prefer single word variable names where possible
- Use Bun APIs when possible, like `Bun.file()`
- Rely on type inference when possible; avoid explicit type annotations or interfaces unless necessary for exports or clarity
- Prefer functional array methods (flatMap, filter, map) over for loops; use type guards on filter to maintain type inference downstream

### Naming

Prefer single word names for variables and functions. Only use multiple words if necessary.

```ts
// Good
const foo = 1
function journal(dir: string) {}

// Bad
const fooBar = 1
function prepareJournal(dir: string) {}
```

Reduce total variable count by inlining when a value is only used once.

```ts
// Good
const journal = await Bun.file(path.join(dir, "journal.json")).json()

// Bad
const journalPath = path.join(dir, "journal.json")
const journal = await Bun.file(journalPath).json()
```

### Destructuring

Avoid unnecessary destructuring. Use dot notation to preserve context.

```ts
// Good
obj.a
obj.b

// Bad
const { a, b } = obj
```

### Variables

Prefer `const` over `let`. Use ternaries or early returns instead of reassignment.

```ts
// Good
const foo = condition ? 1 : 2

// Bad
let foo
if (condition) foo = 1
else foo = 2
```

### Control Flow

Avoid `else` statements. Prefer early returns.

```ts
// Good
function foo() {
  if (condition) return 1
  return 2
}

// Bad
function foo() {
  if (condition) return 1
  else return 2
}
```

### Schema Definitions (Drizzle)

Use snake_case for field names so column names don't need to be redefined as strings.

```ts
// Good
const table = sqliteTable("session", {
  id: text().primaryKey(),
  project_id: text().notNull(),
  created_at: integer().notNull(),
})

// Bad
const table = sqliteTable("session", {
  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
  projectID: text("project_id").notNull(),
  createdAt: integer("created_at").notNull(),
})
```

## Testing

- Avoid mocks as much as possible
- Test actual implementation, do not duplicate logic into tests
- Tests cannot run from repo root (guard: `do-not-run-tests-from-root`); run from package dirs like `packages/opencode`.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

1	# 04 Spec: Runtime Contracts and State Machines
     2	
     3	## Goal
     4	
     5	Establish deterministic runtime contracts for `Run`, `Operation`, `Draft`, and audit/event records so all later phases build on a single, testable persistence model.
     6	
     7	## Why This Phase Exists
     8	
     9	The PRD now defines strict lifecycle and policy behavior. Implementation should not start with ad hoc state updates. We need a contract-first baseline that guarantees:
    10	
    11	- canonical statuses and failure codes
    12	- legal transition edges
    13	- idempotent attempt identities
    14	- auditable event lineage
    15	
    16	Without this phase, later queue/integration/draft features will diverge and become hard to validate.
    17	
    18	## In Scope
    19	
    20	- Add/extend DB schema for:
    21	  - `run` with canonical status enum and reason/failure fields
    22	  - `operation` with linkage fields and status
    23	  - `draft` lifecycle fields and provenance
    24	  - `integration_attempt` identity and replay fields
    25	  - audit/event table with required baseline fields
    26	- Add enum and transition-guard layer in runtime services.
    27	- Add canonical failure code enum values from PRD.
    28	- Enforce illegal-transition rejection at service layer.
    29	- Add deterministic indexes/constraints:
    30	  - uniqueness for `(run_id, integration_attempt_id)`
    31	  - immutable queue ordering key behavior for `ready_for_integration_at`
    32	  - dedupe-oriented lookup indexes needed by later phases
    33	  - policy/dispatch lineage lookup indexes on audit/event records
    34	- Add migration(s) and startup schema compatibility checks.
    35	- Add SDK/runtime types if server contracts are changed.
    36	
    37	Canonical schema contract in this phase must explicitly list:
    38	
    39	- columns per entity (`run`, `operation`, `draft`, `integration_attempt`, `audit_event`)
    40	- constraints (unique, nullable, default, foreign-key expectations)
    41	- indexes and query intent
    42	
    43	Baseline column/constraint floor for this phase:
    44	
    45	- `run`:
    46	  - `id` (uuidv7, pk)
    47	  - `status` (enum, indexed)
    48	  - `trigger_type`
    49	  - `workspace_id`
    50	  - `session_id`
    51	  - `ready_for_integration_at` (immutable after first set)
    52	  - `failure_code` / `reason_code`

    54	- `operation`:

    67	  - policy lineage fields
    68	  - timestamps
    69	- `integration_attempt`:
    70	  - `id` (`integration_attempt_id`, pk)
    71	  - `run_id`
    72	  - `workspace_id`
    73	  - `state` (`attempt_created`, `jj_applied`, `db_linked`, `finalized`)
    74	  - `replay_index`
    75	  - unique `(run_id, id)`
    76	- `audit_event`:

    80	  - policy lineage fields (required for policy/dispatch events)
    81	  - typed `event_payload`
    82	
    83	## Out of Scope
    84	
    85	- JJ bootstrap/workspace operations.
    86	- Workflow execution logic.
    87	- Integration queue processor.
    88	- Draft dispatch behavior.
    89	- Cron/signal trigger behavior.
    90	- New user-facing screens.
    91	
    92	## Baseline Assumptions
    93	
    94	- DB hard-stop behavior on DB failure remains required.
    95	- Drizzle/schema system remains the source of truth for runtime tables.
    96	- Existing runtime can accept additive migrations without destructive reset.
    97	- Runtime IDs in this phase use UUIDv7 only.
    98	- Transition write + event write for one state change happen in one transaction boundary.
    99	- Direct table writes outside transition services are removed or blocked in touched paths.
   100	
   101	## Required Keep Set
   102	
   103	Not applicable.

   121	
   122	## Agent Execution Plan
   123	
   124	### Step 1: Add Canonical Runtime Enums and Failure Codes
   125	
   126	- Introduce shared enums for:
   127	  - `run.status`
   128	  - `operation.status`
   129	  - `draft.status`
   130	  - standardized failure/reason codes
   131	- Ensure enums are reused by DB schema + service validation + API serialization.
   132	
   133	### Step 2: Extend DB Schema and Constraints
   134	
   135	- Add required columns and indexes for PRD contracts:
   136	  - immutable queue ordering key
   137	  - integration attempt identity and replay fields
   138	  - policy lineage fields on relevant events
   139	- Enforce uniqueness and nullability constraints.
   140	- Provide deterministic default values where required.
   141	
   142	### Step 3: Add Transition Guard Service
   143	
   144	- Add centralized transition function per entity.
   145	- Reject illegal transitions with machine-readable errors.
   146	- Record mandatory terminal metadata (`reason`, `failure_code`, timestamps).
   147	- Check legal edges against an explicit in-spec transition matrix and keep matrix synced with tests.
   148	
   149	Canonical legal transition matrix in this phase:
   150	
   151	- `run.status`:
   152	  - `queued -> running|canceled`
   153	  - `running -> validating|failed|canceled`
   154	  - `validating -> ready_for_integration|completed_no_change|failed|canceled`
   155	  - `ready_for_integration -> integrating|canceled`
   156	  - `integrating -> reconciling|completed|failed|cancel_requested`
   157	  - `reconciling -> integrating|failed|cancel_requested`
   158	  - `cancel_requested -> completed|canceled|failed`
   159	  - `completed|completed_no_change|failed|canceled|skipped` are terminal
   160	- `operation.status`:
   161	  - `completed -> reverted`
   162	  - `reverted` terminal
   163	- `draft.status`:
   164	  - `pending -> approved|auto_approved|blocked|rejected`
   165	  - `approved|auto_approved -> sent|failed|blocked|pending` (pending on material edit invalidation)
   166	  - `blocked -> pending|rejected|failed`
   167	  - `sent|rejected|failed` terminal
   168	
   169	### Step 4: Add Audit/Event Baseline Writer
   170	
   171	- Add typed event creation utility that validates required fields by event category.
   172	- Enforce schema-level redaction rules for payload shape (no secret-like free-form fields in baseline typed payload).
   173	
   174	### Step 5: Validation and Regression Coverage
   175	
   176	- Add unit tests for state transitions and forbidden edges.
   177	- Add migration tests for schema evolution.
   178	- Add persistence tests for uniqueness/constraint enforcement.
   179	- Add migration/backfill tests for legacy rows missing newly required fields.
   180	
   181	## Acceptance Criteria
   182	
   183	- `AC-01` Canonical enums exist and are reused across schema and runtime state transitions.
   184	  - Verification: unit tests import the same enum source in DB/runtime modules.
   185	  - Pass condition: no duplicated ad hoc enum strings in transition logic.
   186	
   187	- `AC-02` Illegal transitions are rejected deterministically.
   188	  - Verification: unit tests attempt invalid edges for `run`, `operation`, `draft`.
   189	  - Pass condition: each invalid transition returns machine-readable error and no DB mutation.
   190	
   191	- `AC-03` Full legal-edge coverage exists for canonical transition matrix.
   192	  - Verification: transition tests enumerate all legal edges and expected metadata outputs.
   193	  - Pass condition: no legal edge is untested and no undocumented edge is allowed.
   194	
   195	- `AC-04` Required uniqueness and immutability constraints are enforced.
   196	  - Verification: persistence test inserts duplicate `(run_id, integration_attempt_id)`.
   197	  - Pass condition: duplicate insert fails and attempts to mutate immutable queue key fail.
   198	
   199	- `AC-05` Required audit baseline fields and payload schema/redaction constraints are enforced for applicable event types.
   200	  - Verification: tests attempt writes missing required policy lineage fields for policy/dispatch events.
   201	  - Pass condition: invalid writes are rejected with explicit validation error and secret-like payload fields are blocked.
   202	
   203	- `AC-06` Migration/backfill is forward-applicable on existing local DB state.
   204	  - Verification command:
   205	```bash
   206	bun run --cwd packages/opencode test -- storage
   207	```
   208	  - Pass condition: migration/backfill tests pass and no destructive reset is required.
   209	
   210	- `AC-07` Typecheck/test baseline for touched packages is green.
   211	  - Verification commands:
   212	```bash
   213	bun run --cwd packages/opencode typecheck
   214	bun run --cwd packages/opencode test
   215	bun run --cwd packages/app typecheck
   216	```
   217	  - Pass condition: all commands pass.
   218	
   219	## Risk Register
   220	
   221	- Schema churn may require follow-up migrations if later phases discover missing fields.
   222	- Overly strict constraints can block transitional writes during rollout.
   223	- Poor event payload typing can create frequent migration noise.
   224	- Transition guards can be bypassed if direct table writes remain in legacy paths.
````

1	# 11 Spec: Cron and Signal Trigger Engine
     2	
     3	## Goal
     4	
     5	Implement automated workflow entry via cron and signal triggers with deterministic dedupe/retry/missed-run behavior and clear run recording semantics.
     6	
     7	## Why This Phase Exists
     8	
     9	After manual execution and outbound safety are stable, automation can be enabled safely. Trigger semantics must be deterministic to avoid duplicate work, silent drops, or confusing history.
    10	
    11	## In Scope
    12	
    13	- Cron trigger engine:
    14	  - user timezone scheduling
    15	  - missed-slot behavior (`skipped` + notify)
    16	  - missed-slot expansion cap/coalescing policy for large offline gaps
    17	  - deterministic DST behavior for spring-forward and fall-back boundaries
    18	  - retry policy with retryable/non-retryable classes
    19	- Signal trigger engine:
    20	  - Origin workspace only
    21	  - no backfill on enable
    22	  - dedupe key enforcement
    23	  - duplicate-event recording
    24	  - fallback hash behavior when provider event ID is missing
    25	  - explicit no-backfill boundary rule: process only events with `event_time > signal_enabled_at`
    26	- Trigger-to-run creation pipeline:
    27	  - new session per top-level run
    28	  - run metadata includes trigger source
    29	- Runs visibility for trigger outcomes:
    30	  - skipped cron slots
    31	  - duplicate signal event rows (`Ignored duplicate signal`)
    32	
    33	## Out of Scope
    34	
    35	- Reconciliation long-running reminder UX polish.
    36	- Governance retention tier expansion.
    37	- New integration adapter development.
    38	
    39	## Baseline Assumptions
    40	
    41	- Workflow definitions and manual execution path are stable.
    42	- Deduplication persistence fields exist.
    43	- Notification surfaces exist for skip/duplicate info.
    44	- Trigger failure classes map to canonical machine-readable failure codes.
    45	- Dedupe retention horizon (minimum 30 days) is greater than expected provider replay windows for this phase.
    46	- Missed-slot coalescing policy in this phase:
    47	  - generate at most 100 skipped rows per scheduler catch-up cycle
    48	  - if more than 100 slots are missed, write one coalesced skipped summary row for the overflow
    49	- DST policy in this phase:
    50	  - spring-forward: nonexistent local-time slots are recorded as skipped (`dst_gap_skipped`)
    51	  - fall-back: duplicated local-time slots are evaluated by unique UTC schedule boundaries (can yield two executions where applicable)
    52	- Retry-class mapping in this phase:
    53	  - retryable: `integration_transport_error`, `transient_runtime_error`, `integration_timeout`
    54	  - non-retryable: `validation_error`, `schema_error`, `policy_blocked`, `workspace_policy_blocked`
    55	- Fallback hash canonicalization contract:
    56	  - canonical JSON with lexicographically sorted keys
    57	  - UTF-8 encoding
    58	  - adapter-owned field allowlist version tag included in hash input
    59	
    60	## Required Keep Set
    61	
    62	Not applicable.
    63

    65	
    66	Not applicable.
    67

    72	  - [bus-event.ts](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/packages/opencode/src/bus/bus-event.ts)
    73	  - [util/signal.ts](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/packages/opencode/src/util/signal.ts)

    77	
    78	## Agent Execution Plan
    79	
    80	### Step 1: Implement Cron Scheduling and Missed-Slot Logic
    81	
    82	- Schedule by user timezone.
    83	- On missed windows, record `skipped` run outcome and notify.
    84	- Apply retry policy only for retryable failures.
    85	- Apply missed-slot cap/coalescing when offline gap would create excessive skipped rows.
    86	- Implement explicit DST boundary behavior (spring-forward/fall-back).
    87	- Emit deterministic overflow summary when coalescing is applied.
    88	
    89	### Step 2: Implement Signal Ingress and Dedupe
    90	
    91	- Enforce dedupe uniqueness key.
    92	- If provider event ID absent, compute fallback hash from canonical adapter fields.
    93	- Fallback hash canonicalization uses sorted-key JSON + UTF-8 + adapter field-set version tag.
    94	- Duplicate event records no run execution and creates visible duplicate-event row.
    95	- Reject or ignore events at/before enable timestamp (`event_time > signal_enabled_at`).
    96	
    97	### Step 3: Trigger Metadata and Session Creation
    98

   539	- Debug threshold allowed range: 1..120 minutes.
   540	- Debug hard-stop allowed range: 5..240 minutes.
   541	- Invariant: `hard_stop >= threshold + 1 minute`.
   542	
   543	## UX Requirements
   544	
   545	1. Keep standard user surfaces non-technical.
   546	2. Hide debug/reconciliation sessions by default.
   547	3. Provide linked navigation between History, Runs, Drafts, Sessions, and JJ-linked operation/change records.
   548	4. Preserve clear distinction:
   549	   - Operations: "what changed on disk"
   550	   - Runs: "what executed"
   551	   - Drafts: "what can be sent externally"
   552	
   553	## Security and Trust Guardrails
   554	
   555	1. Integrations/signals only in Origin workspace.
   556	2. No cross-workspace access.
   557	3. Agents never read raw integration API keys. Integration calls are brokered by backend adapters that hold credentials and expose only operation APIs to agents.
   558	4. Outbound integration writes are controlled through drafts.
   559	5. Sandboxing is acknowledged as important and explicitly planned later.
   560	6. Monitoring/polling reads over JJ for runtime telemetry must use read-only invocation patterns to avoid unintended working-copy snapshots.
   561	7. Integration credentials are stored only in OS secure secret storage and are never persisted in workspace files, JJ history, core DB rows, draft payloads, or report artifacts.
   562	8. Secrets must be redacted from diagnostics, crash dumps, agent-visible tool output, and exported report payloads.
   563	9. Runtime polling/telemetry commands must use canonical read-only JJ flags (`--ignore-working-copy --at-op=@`) where supported.
   564	
   565	## Standard Failure Codes
   566	
   567	This phase standardizes these machine-readable outcome codes:
   568	
   569	- `reconciliation_failed`
   570	- `reconciliation_timeout`
   571	- `stale_base_replay_exhausted`
   572	- `cleanup_failed`
   573	- `dispatch_revalidation_failed`
   574	- `duplicate_event`
   575	- `workspace_policy_blocked`
   576	
   577	## Success Metrics
   578	
   579	## Product Health
   580	
   581	1. Share of runs that complete without manual intervention.
   582	2. Share of outbound actions routed through Drafts before send.
   583	3. Time-to-resolution for invalid configuration states.
   584	4. Rate of successful restore flows from Operations.
   585	Metric targets, windows, and pass/fail thresholds are defined in numbered phase specs, not in this PRD.
   586	
   587	## UX Quality
   588	
   589	1. Reduction in user confusion between run state vs file state.
   590	2. Percentage of users who can locate and act on pending drafts without guidance.
   591	3. Drop in visible conflict complexity exposed to non-debug users.
   592	Quantitative UX targets are defined in numbered phase specs.
   593	
   594	## Risks and Mitigations
   595	
   596	1. Complexity creep from automation features.
   597	Mitigation: strict workspace boundaries, workflow-first model, hidden debug complexity.
   598	
   599	2. JJ operation-log/DB divergence concerns.
   600	Mitigation: explicit split of responsibilities, startup reconciliation checks, and strong metadata linking.
   601	
   602	3. Integration duplicate events.
   603	Mitigation: required event dedupe keys in DB.
   604	
   605	4. Invalid hand-edited YAML.
   606	Mitigation: debounced validation, visible errors, and strict non-runnable handling for invalid definitions.
   607	
   608	5. Outbound safety regressions.
   609	Mitigation: Drafts model, default auto-approve OFF, explicit user control.
   610	
   611	## Rollout Guidance
   612	
   613	Implement in incremental phases mapped to specs, with each phase maintaining:
   614	
   615	- Clear acceptance criteria
   616	- Validation commands for touched packages
   617	- Direction updates when scope boundaries change
   618	- Extremely thorough e2e coverage for all new/changed user-facing behavior
   619	
   620	Acceptance criteria and validation are the highest-priority correctness gate in phase specs. New features are not complete without objective, reproducible validation, including e2e tests for happy path, regression path, and negative/error path behavior.
   621	
   622	This PRD should act as the functional and UX baseline for those specs.
   623	
   624	When phase specs discuss JJ operations, changesets, snapshots, or checkpoints, wording must describe app runtime behavior only. Specs must not instruct implementation agents to run VCS history-editing commands or create contribution commits as part of development workflow unless explicitly requested for a code contribution task.
   625	
   626	## Final Scope Boundary Recap
   627	
   628	Included now:
   629	
   630	- Workflow-first automation
   631	- Origin-only integrations/signals
   632	- Unified Library
   633	- Drafts outbound flow
   634	- History split into Operations and Runs
   635	- Hidden automatic reconciliation/debug handling
   636	
   637	Explicitly later:
   638	
   639	- Cloud endpoint operation
   640	- Full sandboxing model
   641	- Multi-user collaboration
```

</details>

 Canonical legal transition matrix in this phase:
 
+- `run.status` creation semantics:
+  - `create -> queued` for executable triggers.
+  - `create -> skipped` allowed only for scheduler-determined non-executable slots (for example missed offline windows or DST spring-forward gaps) and requires non-null `reason_code`.
+  - `create -> skipped` rows are terminal on insert; no session execution is started.
 - `run.status`:
+  - `queued -> skipped` is forbidden (skipped outcomes are direct-create only in this phase contract)
   - `queued -> running|canceled`
   - `running -> validating|failed|canceled`
   - `validating -> ready_for_integration|completed_no_change|failed|canceled`
   - `ready_for_integration -> integrating|canceled`
@@
 - `AC-03` Full legal-edge coverage exists for canonical transition matrix.
-  - Verification: transition tests enumerate all legal edges and expected metadata outputs.
-  - Pass condition: no legal edge is untested and no undocumented edge is allowed.
+  - Verification: transition tests enumerate all legal edges and creation semantics, including `create -> skipped` constraints.
+  - Pass condition: no legal edge is untested, `queued -> skipped` is rejected, and no undocumented edge/path is allowed.
*** End Patch
```

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/11.cron-signal-trigger-engine.spec.md
@@
-- Retry-class mapping in this phase:
+- Retry-class mapping in this phase:
   - retryable: `integration_transport_error`, `transient_runtime_error`, `integration_timeout`
   - non-retryable: `validation_error`, `schema_error`, `policy_blocked`, `workspace_policy_blocked`
+- Persisted code contract for automated trigger failures:
+  - retryable class exhausted after retries:
+    - `run.failure_code=<class_code>`
+    - `run.reason_code=retry_exhausted`
+  - non-retryable class (no retry):
+    - `run.failure_code=<class_code>`
+    - `run.reason_code=non_retryable`
+- Persisted code contract for non-executing trigger outcomes:
+  - cron skipped slot: `run.failure_code` is null and `run.reason_code` is `cron_missed_slot` or `dst_gap_skipped`
+  - duplicate signal event row: `reason_code=duplicate_event` and no run execution counter increment
 - Fallback hash canonicalization contract:
   - canonical JSON with lexicographically sorted keys
   - UTF-8 encoding
   - adapter-owned field allowlist version tag included in hash input
@@
 - Apply retry policy only for retryable failures.
+- Persist terminal run `failure_code` and `reason_code` using the mapping contract in this phase.
 - Apply missed-slot cap/coalescing when offline gap would create excessive skipped rows.
@@
 - `AC-03` Retry policy applies only to retryable classes and canonical failure-code mapping.
   - Verification: failure-class matrix tests.
-  - Pass condition: retryability strictly matches declared class map and failure-code mapping.
+  - Pass condition: retryability strictly matches declared class map, and persisted `run.failure_code`/`run.reason_code` values match the explicit mapping contract for retryable vs non-retryable classes.
*** End Patch
```

### User

What prompt should I give an agent, alongside some spec, to get it to implement it.

I trust you to make the prompt good and achieve the best of the agent's capabilities. There are a few things I want especially:
- the agent should not spare any subagent use, and use subagents whenever it's relevant, would be useful for it, for any kind (explore, research, implement, review, test, debug something); it should have in mind it is an orchestrator and it wants to preserve its context and coordinate subagents
- we want extra focus on validation, for each spec we need EXTREMELY thorough testing, e2e tests, so we can be confident everything was implemented correctly
- I want the agent to know it needs to get back to me if anything is unclear or it's getting stuck to ask for guidance, to avoid workarounds/unclean code that could compound; the highest the quality of every new piece of code, the higher the quality of the code the next agent will generate—it compounds, and we want to do it right, with high standards.


### Reasoning

**Providing master implementation prompt**

### Assistant (final_answer)

Use this as your base prompt (replace placeholders):

```txt
You are the implementation orchestrator for this phase spec:

SPEC: <ABSOLUTE_PATH_TO_PHASE_SPEC_MD>
LOG: <ABSOLUTE_PATH_TO_PHASE_LOG_MD> (create if missing)

Mission:
Implement the spec end-to-end with production quality and zero ambiguity. Treat the spec as binding. Prioritize correctness, clarity, and validation depth over speed.

Critical operating rules:
1. Use subagents aggressively whenever useful.
2. Think like an orchestrator: preserve your own context, delegate scoped work, then integrate.
3. Use subagents for exploration, architecture checks, implementation chunks, code review, testing, debugging, and regression analysis.
4. Prefer multiple focused subagent passes over a single broad pass.
5. Use parallel tool calls whenever tasks are independent.
6. If anything is unclear, contradictory, or under-specified, stop and ask the user targeted questions before coding that part.
7. Do not use workaround/hacky behavior to “force progress”.
8. Treat app-level JJ behavior in specs as product runtime behavior only. Do not confuse that with your own repo workflow actions.

Required process:
1. Read the phase spec fully and extract every Acceptance Criterion (AC) into a checklist.
2. Read direction/process docs and constraints relevant to this repo.
3. Build an implementation plan mapped AC-by-AC.
4. Execute in small increments that stay buildable.
5. After each increment, run the smallest relevant validation.
6. Run a dedicated subagent review pass for correctness and edge cases.
7. Run a dedicated subagent test pass to expand tests until coverage is thorough.
8. Update the phase log with concrete evidence per AC.
9. Finish only when all ACs are explicitly satisfied with proof.

Testing standard (must be extremely thorough):
1. Unit tests for core logic and state transitions.
2. Integration tests for DB/service boundaries.
3. E2E tests for user-visible flows, including negative/error paths and regressions.
4. Determinism/idempotency/concurrency tests where relevant.
5. Validation of failure codes, reason codes, statuses, and recovery behavior.
6. Re-run flaky-prone tests to confirm stability.
7. Never claim done without command-backed evidence.

Quality bar:
1. No “best effort” behavior unless spec explicitly allows it.
2. No silent fallback for critical invariants.
3. Strong typing and clean boundaries.
4. No dead code, TODO shortcuts, or hidden assumptions.
5. Keep implementation understandable for future agents.

Required output at the end:
1. AC-by-AC completion report with exact file references.
2. Test evidence with commands executed and outcomes.
3. Remaining risks (if any) and why they are acceptable.
4. Explicit list of questions/blockers if anything prevented full completion.

Start now.
```

If you want, I can also give you a stricter variant specialized for one specific phase (04, 05, etc.) with phase-specific test expectations baked in.

```text
1	# Agent Guide: Phase Specs and Logs
     2	
     3	This guide defines how agents should document work in `docs/specs/` so every phase is consistent and easy to audit.
     4	
     5	## Purpose
     6	
     7	Use this flow when a human discusses a feature/change with an agent and asks to produce a PRD/spec for a phase.
     8	
     9	The workflow is intentionally split:
    10	
    11	1. Planning phase: human + planning agent co-author the spec.
    12	2. Implementation phase: a separate implementation agent executes the spec.
    13	3. Completion phase: the implementation agent produces the phase log.
    14	
    15	For each phase, agents produce two documents:
    16	
    17	1. A spec (plan before implementation)
    18	2. A log (facts after implementation)
    19	
    20	## Critical Spec Authoring Rules
    21	
    22	These rules apply when a human asks an agent to generate a spec.
    23	
    24	- Push back on anything unclear, contradictory, or underspecified.
    25	- Ask clarifying questions until scope, constraints, and success conditions are explicit.
    26	- Do not write a "ready to implement" spec from vague assumptions.
    27	- If an assumption is unavoidable, mark it explicitly in `## Baseline Assumptions` and call out the risk.
    28	- Treat ambiguity as a blocker, not as permission to guess.
    29	- Do not finalize the spec until unresolved ambiguities are clarified or explicitly accepted as written assumptions.
    30	
    31	## Naming Convention
    32	
    33	Use zero-padded phase numbers and a shared slug.
    34	
    35	- Spec: `NN.<slug>.spec.md`
    36	- Log: `NN.<slug>.log.md`
    37	
    38	Examples:
    39	
    40	- `01.native-app-pruning.spec.md`
    41	- `01.native-app-pruning.log.md`
    42	
    43	Rules:
    44	
    45	- `NN` starts at `01` and increments by 1 per new phase.
    46	- `<slug>` is short, lowercase, and hyphenated.
    47	- Spec and log files for the same phase must use the exact same `NN` and `<slug>`.
    48	
    49	## File Lifecycle
    50	
    51	1. Human and planning agent create the spec first.
    52	2. Implementation agent reads the spec and implements the phase according to it.
    53	3. Implementation agent creates the log with timestamp, commands, changed files, deviations from spec, and validation results.
    54	
    55	Handoff rule:
    56	
    57	- The implementation agent must treat `NN.<slug>.spec.md` as the source of truth for scope, validation, and acceptance criteria.
    58	- At the end of implementation, producing `NN.<slug>.log.md` is mandatory.
    59	
    60	## Required Spec Structure
    61	
    62	Each `NN.<slug>.spec.md` must include these sections in this order:
    63	
    64	1. `# NN Spec: <Title>`
    65	2. `## Goal`
    66	3. `## Why This Phase Exists`
    67	4. `## In Scope`
    68	5. `## Out of Scope`
    69	6. `## Baseline Assumptions`
    70	7. `## Required Keep Set` (if pruning/selection applies)
    71	8. `## Deletion Candidates` (if pruning applies)
    72	9. `## Evidence Anchors` (files or references that justify assumptions)
    73	10. `## Agent Execution Plan` (step-by-step)
    74	11. `## Acceptance Criteria`
    75	12. `## Risk Register`
    76	
    77	Notes:
    78	
    79	- Keep wording specific and testable.
    80	- Include concrete commands in plan/validation sections when possible.
    81	- If a section does not apply, keep the heading and write `Not applicable` with a short reason.
    82	- `## Acceptance Criteria` is the most important section for implementation correctness.
    83	
    84	## Validation and Acceptance Quality Bar
    85	
    86	Validation and acceptance criteria must be bullet-proof. This is the primary mechanism to verify implementation quality.
    87	
    88	- Every acceptance criterion must be objective, binary, and reproducible.
    89	- Every validation item should include:
    90	  - exact command/check to run
    91	  - expected output or observable result
    92	  - explicit pass/fail condition
    93	- Cover functional correctness, regressions, and critical risks from the spec.
    94	- If a criterion is not measurable, rewrite it until it is measurable.
    95	- The implementation agent should be able to determine "done" from this section alone.
    96	- Baseline expectation for implementation phases: all relevant automated tests in touched packages pass.
    97	- New or changed features must include thorough automated coverage for happy path, regression path, and negative/error path behavior.
    98	- For app e2e coverage, prefer the local harness command (`bun run --cwd packages/app test:e2e:local ...`) in acceptance criteria.
    99	- Bun invocation note: for package scripts, put `--cwd` after `run` (for example `bun run --cwd packages/app test:unit`). In some Bun versions, `bun --cwd ... run ...` prints usage and does not execute the script.
   100	- Do not require raw `test:e2e` in acceptance criteria unless the spec also defines explicit backend bootstrap steps for that run mode.
   101	
   102	## Required Log Structure
   103	
   104	Each `NN.<slug>.log.md` must include:
   105	
   106	1. `# NN Execution Log`
   107	2. Timestamp (UTC ISO-8601)
   108	3. Branch
   109	4. `## Commands Run`
   110	5. `## Files Removed` (or `None`)
   111	6. `## Files Edited` (or `None`)
   112	7. `## Deviations from Spec` (or `None`)
   113	8. `## Validation`
   114	9. `## Open Risks`
   115	
   116	Validation format:
   117	
   118	- One line per check with `PASS` or `FAIL`.
   119	- For failures, include exact reason/blocker.
   120	
   121	## Updating Existing Docs
   122	
   123	When changing naming conventions or phase structure:
   124	
   125	- Rename existing files to this convention.
   126	- Update internal links/references to renamed files.
   127	- Preserve historical facts; only adjust wording/headers where needed for consistency.
   128	
   129	## Agent Operating Rules
   130	
   131	- Do not mix planning and log facts in one section.
   132	- Keep commands and outcomes factual; do not hide failed steps.
   133	- If you are generating a spec, challenge unclear requirements immediately and resolve them before finalizing.
   134	- If implementation differs from plan, capture that in `## Deviations from Spec` in the log.
   135	- Prefer additive updates over rewriting history.
   136	- If you are the implementation agent, do not finish the task without creating/updating the phase log file.
   137	
   138	## Quick Start Template
   139	
   140	Use this checklist for a new phase:
   141	
   142	- Pick next phase number `NN`.
   143	- Define slug `<slug>`.
   144	- Interrogate unclear requirements first; finalize nothing until scope and success criteria are explicit.
   145	- Create `docs/specs/NN.<slug>.spec.md` with required sections.
   146	- Implement work.
   147	- Create `docs/specs/NN.<slug>.log.md` with required log facts.
   148	- Update `docs/specs/00.project-direction.md` if the project direction or scope boundaries changed.
```

</details>

### Assistant (final_answer)

Mostly clear, but not from each spec alone.

- The log contract is defined centrally in [GUIDE.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/GUIDE.md:102), including exact required sections and `PASS`/`FAIL` validation format.
- It is also explicitly mandatory for implementation agents in [GUIDE.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/GUIDE.md:136).
- The phase specs (04–14) do not each repeat a per-spec “how to write the log” section.

So: if the agent reads spec + guide, yes it’s clear. If you hand only one spec file, it’s not fully self-contained for log formatting.

### User

Maybe we can tell it to read the guide in that prompt so it knows how to use a spec?

Also I'm wondering if the sentence "Treat the spec as binding" might not be counter productive. What do you think? My thinking is that we do definitely want to keep the agent in sync with what we want, but also I'm worried this might kill the ability of the agent to shine, for instance if they think they can do something better, and they don't because the spec is binding.

What is your opinion?


### Reasoning

**Crafting refined prompt guidance**

### Assistant (final_answer)

Yes, add that explicitly. It will reduce drift.

And your concern is valid: “Treat the spec as binding” is good for contracts, but bad if interpreted as “don’t improve anything.”

Best formulation is:

```txt
Treat the spec as binding for WHAT (scope, invariants, acceptance criteria, safety boundaries).
You are free to optimize HOW (architecture, decomposition, implementation details) as long as all acceptance criteria remain satisfied.
If you think user-visible behavior, contracts, or acceptance criteria should change, stop and propose a spec delta before implementing.
```

Also add this at the top of your prompt:

```txt
Before implementation, read:
1) docs/specs/GUIDE.md (spec/log workflow and log format)
2) docs/specs/00.project-direction.md (global boundaries)
3) the target phase spec
```

This gives the agent both discipline and room to “shine” safely.

1	# Project Direction
     2	
     3	## What This Repo Is
     4	
     5	This repository is a fork of `opencode` focused on building the `origin` native desktop app.
     6	
     7	## Scope Direction
     8	
     9	This fork is desktop-first. We avoid web/cloud/enterprise scope unless an approved phase spec explicitly expands direction.
    10	
    11	Project direction baseline currently includes:
    12	
    13	- [01.native-app-pruning.spec.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/01.native-app-pruning.spec.md)
    14	- [01.native-app-pruning.log.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/01.native-app-pruning.log.md)
    15	- [02.global-entry-workspace.spec.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/02.global-entry-workspace.spec.md)
    16	- [02.global-entry-workspace.log.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/02.global-entry-workspace.log.md)
    17	- [03.origin-namespace-imports-branding.spec.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/03.origin-namespace-imports-branding.spec.md)
    18	- [03.origin-namespace-imports-branding.log.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/03.origin-namespace-imports-branding.log.md)
    19	- [04.runtime-contracts-and-state-machines.spec.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/04.runtime-contracts-and-state-machines.spec.md)
    20	- [04.runtime-contracts-and-state-machines.log.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/04.runtime-contracts-and-state-machines.log.md)
    21	- [05.jj-runtime-foundation.spec.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/05.jj-runtime-foundation.spec.md)
    22	- [05.jj-runtime-foundation.log.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/05.jj-runtime-foundation.log.md)
    23	- [06.workflow-library-authoring-and-validation.spec.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/06.workflow-library-authoring-and-validation.spec.md)
    24	- [06.workflow-library-authoring-and-validation.log.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/06.workflow-library-authoring-and-validation.log.md)
    25	- [07.manual-run-execution.spec.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/07.manual-run-execution.spec.md)
    26	- [07.manual-run-execution.log.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/07.manual-run-execution.log.md)
    27	- [08.integration-queue-and-recovery.spec.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/08.integration-queue-and-recovery.spec.md)
    28	- [08.integration-queue-and-recovery.log.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/08.integration-queue-and-recovery.log.md)
    29	
    30	## Current Direction Baseline
    31	
    32	- Repository remains pruned to desktop-relevant surfaces (Phase `01`).
    33	- App startup defaults into a protected global workspace (`~/Documents/origin` by default) with global entry session flow (Phase `02`).
    34	- Runtime/settings namespace is Origin-owned by default with no implicit OpenCode sharing; OpenCode portability is explicit import-only for providers/auth and model visibility toggles (Phase `03`).
    35	- Runtime persistence/state transitions are machine-enforced with explicit transition legality and policy-lineage audit requirements (Phase `04`).
    36	- JJ runtime operations are adapter-bound with deterministic bootstrap/read policy/run-workspace cleanup semantics and no-snapshot read guardrails (Phase `05`).
    37	- Workflow/library resources are YAML-authored with deterministic validation codes, standard-workspace capability blocking for unsupported resources/triggers, deterministic knowledge-base collision policy, and runtime run-gate rejection for non-runnable workflow definitions (Phase `06`).
    38	- Manual workflow execution is orchestrated with per-run session linkage, deterministic duplicate-trigger rejection, bounded repair retries, run-workspace isolation/cleanup, and integration-candidate staging metadata persistence for change paths (Phase `07`).
    39	- Integration is queue-orchestrated per workspace with immutable ready ordering, recovery/finalization of interrupted attempts, single stale-base replay, and deterministic cancel/timeout race settlement semantics (Phase `08`).
    40	- User-facing branding should be `origin` for this app, with explicit upstream exceptions such as `OpenCode Zen` and provider IDs.
    41	
    42	## How Work Is Tracked
    43	
    44	Direction and implementation changes are tracked as numbered phases in `docs/specs/`:
    45	
    46	- `NN.<slug>.spec.md`: planned scope and acceptance criteria
    47	- `NN.<slug>.log.md`: implemented work and validation outcomes
    48	
    49	Process and format rules are defined in:
    50	
    51	- [GUIDE.md](/Users/polarzero/code/projects/origin/docs/specs/GUIDE.md)
    52	
    53	## Current State
    54	
    55	- Repository baseline is a native-app-focused fork after Phase `07`.
    56	- New work should be additive on top of this baseline and documented as new phases.
    57	
    58	## Guardrails For Agents
    59	
    60	- Read this file first for project intent and scope boundaries.
    61	- Do not reintroduce removed non-desktop scope unless an approved phase spec explicitly requires it.
    62	- Do not reintroduce implicit OpenCode fallback/sync or broad auto-migration behavior without an approved phase spec.
    63	- Do not remove/bypass protected global workspace behavior without an approved phase spec.
    64	- Keep changes aligned with native desktop goals and phase acceptance criteria.
    65	
    66	## Update Rule
    67	
    68	Update this document only when project direction or scope boundaries change.
    69	Implementation details and command history belong in phase `.spec` and `.log` files.
```

```text
1	# PRD: Origin Agentic Workflows and Automation
     2	
     3	## Document Status
     4	
     5	- Status: Draft for product alignment
     6	- Date: 2026-03-05
     7	- Product: `origin` native desktop app
     8	- Audience: Product, design, engineering
     9	- Execution note: This PRD is design input. Implementation must still be planned and executed via numbered phase specs/logs in `docs/specs/` per `docs/specs/GUIDE.md`.
    10	- Critical scope rule: any `jj`/changeset/checkpoint language in this PRD describes app runtime behavior in user workspaces. It does not instruct implementation agents to run VCS commands in this repository unless explicitly requested for a code contribution task.
    11	
    12	## Summary
    13	
    14	Origin should evolve from a chat-first coding surface into a local-first personal operations app where users can define workflows, run them manually or automatically, and review all effects safely. The system must stay understandable for non-developers while preserving strong auditability and control.
    15	
    16	This PRD defines a workflow-first model with:
    17	
    18	- Local desktop execution only (no cloud runtime in this phase)
    19	- Single-user operation
    20	- JJ-tracked workspace state (changesets + operation log) for restorable user-facing changes
    21	- Database-backed runtime/control-plane state
    22	- Origin-only integrations/signals
    23	- Outbound draft review before external writes
    24	
    25	The product objective is to make automation powerful and reliable without exposing users to VCS complexity, conflict resolution mechanics, or unsafe external side effects.
    26	
    27	## Problem Statement
    28	
    29	Today, users can prompt agents in chat, but recurring and structured work lacks first-class primitives. There is no coherent model for:
    30	
    31	- Scheduled recurring execution
    32	- External-event-triggered execution
    33	- Reusable resources for workflows
    34	- Human review gates for outbound actions
    35	- Clear separation between "what changed on disk" and "what happened at runtime"
    36	
    37	This leads to fragmented behavior, weak auditability, and unclear UX when automation grows beyond ad hoc chats. Users need predictable automation with understandable history and strong control over external effects.
    38	
    39	## Why This Matters
    40	
    41	Users want to:
    42	
    43	- Capture intent once and rerun safely
    44	- See what changed and revert it easily
    45	- Trust that external integrations do not act without explicit control
    46	- Keep all behavior local, private, and inspectable
    47	
    48	If Origin provides this in a consumer-friendly way, it can become a dependable daily operations layer, not just a chat surface.
    49	
    50	## Product Principles
    51	
    52	1. Local-first and explicit: no hidden cloud behavior.
    53	2. Workflow-first: triggerable structured units of automation.
    54	3. Safe outbound model: agent proposes drafts, user controls sending.
    55	4. Human-readable state: YAML and Markdown where appropriate.
    56	5. Auditability without overload: clean operation history plus run telemetry.
    57	6. Hidden complexity: advanced reconciliation/debug sessions run automatically.
    58	7. Strong boundaries: no cross-workspace access; integrations only in Origin workspace.
    59	8. Baseline preservation: existing accepted direction from phase `02` and phase `03` remains in effect unless a future phase spec explicitly supersedes it.
    60	
    61	## Goals
    62	
    63	1. Enable users to create and run workflows via manual, cron, and signal triggers.
    64	2. Provide reusable Library resources (`query`, `script`, `prompt_template`) with clear "used by" relationships.
    65	3. Ensure file mutations are JJ-tracked, operation-linked, and restorable.
    66	4. Track all runtime activity in DB (`Runs`, `Drafts`, status/notifications).
    67	5. Prevent direct outbound side effects by default through a Drafts inbox.
    68	6. Keep non-origin workspaces useful but intentionally constrained.
    69	7. Keep UX simple for average users while retaining full traceability.
    70	
    71	## Non-Goals
    72	
    73	1. Cloud-hosted execution or remote control endpoints.
    74	2. Multi-user collaboration and shared ownership semantics.
    75	3. Cross-workspace read/write automation.
    76	4. Full sandboxing in this phase (noted as future requirement).
    77	5. Manual terminal-based VCS workflow support for end users.
    78	6. Best-effort fallback operation when DB is unavailable.
    79	
    80	## Target Users
    81	
    82	### Primary
    83	
    84	- Individuals using Origin as a local personal operations app.
    85	- Users who want automation power without needing deep VCS knowledge.
    86	
    87	### Secondary
    88	
    89	- Power users who inspect history, customize workflows/resources, and debug behavior.
    90	
    91	## Scope by Workspace Type
    92	
    93	### Origin Workspace
    94	
    95	The Origin workspace is the only workspace with integrations and signals.
    96	
    97	Views:
    98	
    99	- History
   100	- Drafts
   101	- Workflows
   102	- Library
   103	- Integrations
   104	- Calendar
   105	- Notes
   106	- Knowledge Base
   107	
   108	### Non-Origin Workspaces
   109	
   110	Constrained local automation, no integrations/signals/outbound drafts.
   111	
   112	Views:
   113	
   114	- History
   115	- Workflows
   116	- Library
   117	- Knowledge Base
   118	
   119	Workspace type is selected at creation (`origin` or `standard`) and is immutable in this phase.
   120	Workspace type must be visible in workspace settings.
   121	
   122	## Information Architecture
   123	
   124	## History
   125	
   126	Tabs:
   127	
   128	- Operations: app-level mutation history linked to JJ operation IDs and resulting changesets
   129	- Runs: all execution attempts and outcomes
   130	
   131	Operations and Runs must cross-link where relevant.
   132	Default `Operations` filter shows app-authored entries. A source toggle allows including user-authored entries.
   133	Minimum cross-link contract:
   134	- Operation rows linked to a run expose `Open run`.
   135	- Runs with file changes expose `Open operation`.
   136	- Runs that produced drafts expose `Open draft(s)`.
   137	
   138	## Drafts
   139	
   140	Tabs:
   141	
   142	- Pending
   143	- Processed
   144	
   145	Drafts represent outbound external actions proposed by agents and controlled by the user.
   146	
   147	## Core Concepts
   148	
   149	### Workflow
   150	
   151	A declarative automation definition with:
   152	
   153	- Trigger (`manual`, `cron`, `signal`)
   154	- Steps / instructions
   155	- References to Library resources
   156	
   157	Workflows are the top-level automation object.
   158	
   159	### Run
   160	
   161	A single execution instance of a workflow or trigger context. Runs are DB records and always link to a chat session.
   162	
   163	### Operation
   164	
   165	A run may produce file mutations. If file changes exist, integration records one app operation for the full top-level run and links it to the corresponding JJ operation entry and resulting changeset IDs.
   166	
   167	### Library Resource
   168	
   169	Unified reusable resource registry with kinds:
   170	
   171	- `query`
   172	- `script`
   173	- `prompt_template`
   174	
   175	Availability:
   176	
   177	- Origin workspace: all kinds
   178	- Non-origin workspace: `script`, `prompt_template` only
   179	
   180	### Draft
   181	
   182	A DB record representing a proposed outbound integration action. User can edit, approve, send, reject, or open linked session.
   183	
   184	## Data and Storage Model
   185	
   186	## Source of Truth Split
   187	
   188	- JJ repository state: versioned changesets and operation log
   189	- JJ workspace state: working-copy content and workspace metadata
   190	- DB: runtime/control-plane state (runs, drafts, statuses, notifications, links)
   191	
   192	DB availability is mandatory. If DB is unavailable or corrupt, app enters blocking error state and cannot proceed.
   193	Blocking screen must provide these actions:
   194	- `Retry`
   195	- `Open diagnostics`
   196	- `Recovery steps`
   197	
   198	## Data Retention
   199	
   200	Retention is split to keep user history durable while bounding telemetry growth.
   201	
   202	- Core product data is retained without automatic expiry in this phase:
   203	  - workspaces, sessions, runs, operations, drafts, workflows, library resources, settings, linkage records
   204	- Audit/event logs are pruned with default retention of 30 days:
   205	  - lifecycle transitions, policy decisions, notification events, watchdog/debug events
   206	- Integration idempotency keys (`integration_attempt_id`) are retained for at least 90 days to avoid duplicate side effects after crashes/retries.
   207	
   208	## File Layout
   209	
   210	- `.origin/workflows/*.yaml`
   211	- `.origin/library/*.yaml`
   212	- `.origin/knowledge-base/**`
   213	- `calendar/YYYY-MM.yaml` (Origin workspace only)
   214	- `notes/**/*.md` (Origin workspace only)
   215	
   216	All YAML definitions include `schema_version`.
   217	
   218	## JJ Runtime Behavior (App-Managed)
   219	
   220	- The app bundles and uses a pinned `jj` runtime for all product operations. Runtime behavior must not depend on users having `jj` installed globally.
   221	- Auto-bootstrap JJ metadata in workspaces:
   222	  - If workspace already has JJ metadata, do nothing.
   223	  - If workspace has `.git` but no JJ metadata, initialize JJ in colocated mode without modifying existing git history.
   224	  - If workspace has no VCS metadata, initialize JJ metadata in place without requiring terminal setup.
   225	- Identity write rules (JJ config, repo/workspace-local scope only):
   226	  - If both are missing, set `user.name=origin` and `user.email=origin@local`.
   227	  - If only one field is missing, set only the missing field.
   228	  - Never overwrite existing local identity values.
   229	  - Never write user/global config.
   230	- Create Operations entries only when file content changed.
   231	- No-change runs do not create Operations entries.
   232	- User-authored and app-authored history entries appear in Operations with filtering.
   233	
   234	## Operation Metadata
   235	
   236	Canonical operation linkage fields are stored in DB and linked to JJ identifiers:
   237	
   238	- `operation_id`
   239	- `session_id`
   240	- `run_id`
   241	- `trigger_type`
   242	- `workspace_id`
   243	- `workflow_id` (when applicable)
   244	- `integration_attempt_id`
   245	- `ready_for_integration_at` (immutable after first enqueue)
   246	- `jj_base_change_id`
   247	- `jj_result_change_ids`
   248	- `jj_operation_ids` (ordered append-only list in execution order)
   249	- `jj_operation_phases` (same-length ordered phase labels for `jj_operation_ids`)
   250	- `jj_commit_ids`
   251	- `changed_paths`
   252	- `source_operation_id` (when operation reverts a prior operation)
   253	- `integration_head_change_id_before_apply`
   254	- `integration_head_change_id_after_apply`
   255	
   256	ID format: UUIDv7.
   257	
   258	## State Models
   259

   270	
   271	## Audit Event Baseline
   272	
   273	Audit events are DB records distinct from core product entities.
   274	
   275	Required categories in this phase:
   276	
   277	- run lifecycle transitions
   278	- integration attempt lifecycle
   279	- reconciliation/debug watchdog notifications and terminal outcomes
   280	- draft lifecycle transitions
   281	- policy decisions for auto-approve and destination/action constraints
   282	- outbound dispatch attempt/result
   283	- security-impacting setting changes (for example auto-approve toggles)
   284	
   285	Required fields per event:
   286	
   287	- `event_id`
   288	- `occurred_at`
   289	- `workspace_id`
   290	- `session_id`
   291	- `run_id` (when applicable)
   292	- `operation_id` (when applicable)
   293	- `draft_id` (when applicable)
   294	- `integration_id` (when applicable)
   295	- `integration_attempt_id` (when applicable)
